title: Benjamin Bolker
text:
McMaster University


Short Course Title Generalized Mixed Models: Basics and New Frontiers

title: Benjamin Bolker
text:
Short bio
Benjamin Bolker, PhD, is a professor in the departments of Mathematics & Statistics and Biology at McMaster University.
Professor Bolker has broad interests in computational statistics and population biology including mixed models, infectious disease dynamics, evolution of virulence, animal movement, and spatial population dynamics. He has published more than 100 peer-reviewed publications (including one listed in the top 25 most-cited papers of the 21st century) as well the book Ecological Models and Data in R, which has been a gateway to advanced statistical modeling for many ecologists, and (with Dr. Marta Wayne) a Very Short Introduction to Infectious Disease (Oxford University Press) for general readers.
He is also well known for supporting researchers using mixed models and doing statistical analyses in ecology through his development and maintenance of widely used R packages (lme4, glmmTMB, bbmle), with millions of downloads and tens of thousands of literature citations, as well as answering questions in forums such as Stack Overflow. He directs the School of Computational Science and Engineering at McMaster and serves as associate chair (graduate) for Mathematics. He is a long-time associate editor for the leading ecology and evolution journal American Naturalist.

title: Christel Faes
text: Universiteit Hasselt

title: Christel Faes
text:
Short bio
Christel Faes is a Belgian biostatistician and Full Professor of Statistics at the Center for Statistics and the Data Science Institute at Hasselt University. She obtained an MSc in Mathematics from the University of Antwerp in 2000 and a PhD in Statistics from Hasselt University in 2004. Her research connects statistical methodology with questions in public health, environmental health, and disease prevention. She works in the field of spatial and spatio-temporal statistics, developing methods that help reveal how health risks vary across regions, populations, and time.
Her work has contributed to COVID-19 modelling, climate-health research, cancer surveillance, and the assessment of heat-related health impacts. During the COVID-19 pandemic, she contributed statistical modelling expertise to the Belgian response, and she advises Flemish initiatives on climate and health. She has published more than 230 peer-reviewed articles and has supervised numerous PhD and master’s students. In addition to her research, she serves as UHasselt Director of the Interuniversity Institute of Biostatistics, a joint research institute of UHasselt and KU Leuven.

title: Joaquín Martínez-Minaya
text: Universitat Poltècnica de València

title: Joaquín Martínez-Minaya
text:
Short bio
My name is Joaquín Martínez-Minaya, and I am an Associate Professor in the Department of Applied Statistics and Operational Research and Quality at the Universitat Politècnica de València (Spain). My research lies in the field of Applied Bayesian Statistics, where I develop statistical methods for the analysis of multivariate, compositional, spatial and spatio-temporal data. My work combines methodological developments in Bayesian inference with applications in environmental health, ecology and biomedicine. I am interested in hierarchical models, latent Gaussian models, compositional data analysis, measurement agreement, model assessment, validation and predictive performance, and species distribution modelling. From a computational perspective, my research relies on Bayesian inference techniques, especially Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA) and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo methods.

title: IWSM2027
text:
International Workshop on Statistical Modelling


Bilbao 2027
The Department of Mathematics of the University of the Basque Country (EHU) is pleased to host the 41st International Workshop on Statistical Modelling (IWSM) at the Bizkaia Aretoa, Bilbao, Spain, from Sunday 18th July to Friday 23rd July 2027.
IWSM is the world’s leading workshop dedicated to statistical modelling and one of the main activities of the Statistical Modelling Society. The workshop focuses on novel statistical modelling developments aimed at solving real-world problems. The atmosphere at the workshop is friendly and supportive, and, with no parallel sessions, IWSM offers an excellent opportunity for knowledge exchange among all participants.
Submissions to the workshop take the form of 4-page proceedings papers, enabling participants to share their ideas in sufficient detail. All papers are subject to a rigorous review process to ensure high-quality contributions in both the oral and poster sessions. We particularly encourage submissions from postgraduate students and early-career researchers.

title: Accommodation
text:
Bilbao offers a wide range of hotels in various categories (Figure 1 shows some of them). Given the high number of tourists in July, it is highly recommended to book your accommodation as early as possible. We are currently in contact with various hotels in the city to secure discounts for conference attendees. Additionally, we have reached out to university residences to reserve rooms at significantly lower rates than those of hotels. Information about potential discounts for conference attendees will be available as soon as possible.







Figure 1: Some of the hotels in Bilbao (provided by Bilbao Convention Bureau).

title: Welcome to Bilbao!
text:
Bilbao, located in northern Spain, is the largest city in the province of Biscay and the Basque Country. It is known for successfully blending traditional Basque culture with a modern vision for the future, making it a must-visit for design and culture enthusiasts. The city is surrounded by green hills and is close to the coast.
Bilbao has a strong devotion to gastronomy, with a tradition of celebrating any occasion around good food and drink. There are three main areas for “poteo” (bar hopping) and “pintxos” (Basque tapas): Casco Viejo, Indautxu (García Rivero or Licenciado Pozas streets), and Ensanche (Ledesma or Diputación streets). Each bar offers a unique style, and attendees are encouraged to explore them. Bilbao is a relatively small city and can easily be explored on foot, without the need for public transport.

title: Welcome to Bilbao!
text:
University of the Basque Country (EHU)
The University of the Basque Country, officially known as Euskal Herriko Unibertsitatea (EHU), stands as the most important higher education institution in the Basque Country. As a public university with deep roots in Basque culture, it has trained over 300 000 graduates across all areas of knowledge and is responsible for \(70\%\) of the research carried out in the region. The EHU is characterized by its distributed structure, spanning three main campuses — Bizkaia, Gipuzkoa, and Araba — each corresponding to one of the three Basque provinces. This enables it to offer a wide array of over 70 degrees and 150 graduate programs, with teaching conducted in Basque, Spanish, and an increasing number of courses in English, reflecting its commitment to internationalization.
The Faculty of Science and Technology is one of the largest centers within the EHU, located on the main Campus of Biscay in Leioa, just 14 km from Bilbao. It is a vibrant hub for scientific and technological education and research, offering a diverse range of undergraduate and postgraduate programs, including Biology, Biochemistry, Biotechnology, Chemistry, Chemical Engineering, Electronic Engineering, Geology, Mathematics, and Physics, along with a double degree in Electronic Engineering and Physics. Within this esteemed faculty, the Department of Mathematics plays a crucial role. It encompasses teaching and research across all areas of mathematics, from pure mathematics to applied mathematics, statistics, and operational research. The department is actively involved in both undergraduate and graduate studies, contributing significantly to the academic and research landscape of the university and beyond.

title: Welcome to Bilbao!
text:
Conference Venue
The conference will be held at the Bizkaia Aretoa (Figure 1), part of the University of the Basque Country EHU. This L-shaped building is situated in an emblematic area of Bilbao, offering views of the estuary and the Guggenheim Museum, right in the heart of Avenida de Abandoibarra.







Figure 1: Bizkaia Aretoa building




The Bizkaia Aretoa spans over 9 000 square meters across six floors. The ground floor, covering 2 300 square meters, houses the Mitxelena Auditorium (Figure 2), which can accommodate 441 people. It also includes a large 400 square meter hall, two meeting rooms, and a spacious shop. The first, second, and third floors each have over 1 000 square meters of space, featuring two additional auditoriums for 161 and 66 people, five open spaces for exhibitions, and offices for various EHU services.
Oral presentations will be held in the Mitxelena Auditorium.







Figure 2: Mitxelena Auditorium




Coffee breaks and poster sessions will be held in Axular and Chillida (Figure 3 and Figure 4) exhibition galleries. The rooms Barandiaran and Elhuyar are also booked for the EC Meetings and Scientific Committee meetings (Figure 5). If the weather permits, we can also enjoy the coffee break on the terrace (Figure 6).







Figure 3: Axular Exhibition Gallery (110 \(m^2\))









Figure 4: Chillida Exhibition Gallery (220 \(m^2\))









Figure 5: Barandiaran (on the left) can accommodate up to 16 people. The Elhuyar room (on the right), can accommodate up to 24 people.









Figure 6: The Terrace at the Bizkaia Aretoa




The map in Figure 7 and the picture in Figure 8 show the venue’s location (Bizkaia Aretoa), nearby points of interest, and its connections via metro and tram.








Figure 7: Map of the location of the venue (Bizkaia Aretoa) and connections (provided by Bilbao Convention Bureau).










Figure 8: Bizkaia Aretoa and other points of interest

title: General Information
text:
Bilbao is the most well-connected city in northern Spain.


Bilbao Airport (IATA: BIO) is the most important airport in the region, offering direct flights to major cities in Spain and many European destinations, as well as a direct flight to New York (EWR). The airport is only 15 km from Bilbao city center, a 10-minute car ride. A bus service runs every 30 minutes from the city center, and taxis from the airport to downtown Bilbao cost approximately‚ 25 - 30 euros.
Airlines flying from Bilbao Airport include:

Major Carriers: Lufthansa, Air France, Iberia, Air Europa, British Airways, KLM, TAP Portugal, Swiss, Turkish Airlines, United Airlines, Brussels Airlines, Aer Lingus.
Low-Cost Carriers: Vueling, Volotea, easyJet, Eurowings, Wizz Air, Ryanair (from Santander).

Bilbao Airport has direct flights to over 60 destinations in 20 countries. Some popular direct flight destinations from Europe include: Amsterdam, Basel, Berlin, Bristol, Brussels, Copenhagen, Cork, Dublin, Dusseldorf, Edinburgh, Eindhoven, Frankfurt, Geneva, Hamburg, Istanbul, Lisbon, London (Gatwick, Heathrow), Manchester, Milan, Munich, Naples, Oslo, Paris (Charles de Gaulle, Orly), Porto, Prague, Rome, Stuttgart, Venice, Vienna, and Zurich.
Direct flights from Spain include: Alicante, Barcelona, Fuerteventura, Gran Canaria, Granada, Ibiza, Lanzarote, Madrid, Malaga, Menorca, Palma de Mallorca, Santiago de Compostela, Seville, Tenerife, and Valencia.
Santander Airport (SDR), located about 90 km away, is another viable option, particularly for Ryanair flights. While there are currently no direct flights from Santander to Bilbao, there are good bus connections available.







Figure 1: Map of the connections by plane (provided by Bilbao Convention Bureau).







Thanks to its strategic location, Bilbao enjoys strong transport links by rail and motorway with major cities throughout Spain and Europe. In addition, its port provides maritime connections with the United Kingdom.







Figure 2: Map of the connections by rail and motorways (provided by Bilbao Convention Bureau).

title: Social Program
text:
Local excursions
Attendees will have the opportunity to participate in various local excursions to experience the rich culture and stunning landscapes of the Basque Country. Three exciting options will be available. Further details regarding booking, specific timings, and pricing for these excursions will be provided soon.

Visit to a Txakoli Winery and San Juan de Gaztelugatxe: This excursion will combine the natural beauty of San Juan de Gaztelugatxe with a taste of local winemaking tradition.

San Juan de Gaztelugatxe: A breathtaking islet on the Basque coast (Figure 3), famous for its hermitage dedicated to Saint John the Baptist. Connected to the mainland by a winding stone bridge, visitors can ascend 241 steps to reach the hermitage, ringing its bell three times for good luck and to make a wish. The site offers spectacular views of the Bay of Biscay.








Figure 3: San Juan de Gaztelugatxe





Txakoli Tasting: Following the visit to Gaztelugatxe, attendees will enjoy a tour and tasting at a local txakoli museum Txakoli (pronounced “cha-ko-LEE”) is a unique, lightly carbonated white wine typical of the Basque Country. Its history dates back to Roman times, with its current form developing in the 16th and 17th centuries. Traditionally, a homemade wine consumed in rural farmhouses, it gained popularity in bars by the late 19th century. The quality and authenticity of Txakoli have been protected by Denominations of Origin (DOs) since the late 1980s, such as Getariako Txakolina, Bizkaiko Txakolina, and Arabako Txakolina. Made primarily from indigenous grape varieties like Hondarrabi Zuri, it is characterized by its freshness, acidity, and light fruity notes, making it an ideal pairing for local seafood and pintxos. It is traditionally served chilled and poured from a height to enhance its effervescence.

Boat Trip along the Ría de Bilbao: Attendees can experience Bilbao from a unique perspective with a relaxing boat trip along the Ría del Nervión (Figure 4).







Figure 4: Boat Trip along the Ría del Nervión with Bilboats (photo: Bilboats).




Guided Tour of the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao: Attendees can immerse themselves in the artistic heart of Bilbao with a guided tour of the iconic Guggenheim Museum (Figure 5). Designed by Frank Gehry, this architectural masterpiece houses an impressive collection of modern and contemporary art. A guided tour typically lasts between 1.5 to 2 hours and provides in-depth information about the building’s design, the artworks on display, and the museum’s significance to Bilbao’s urban regeneration.







Figure 5: Guggenheim Bilbao Museum

title: Rhian Daniel
text: Cardiff University

title: Rhian Daniel
text:
Short bio
Rhian Daniel is Professor of Statistics at Cardiff University, Wales, UK. Having studied mathematics at Queens’ College, Cambridge, with a Part III in Statistics, Rhian moved to the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, first to study an MSc in Medical Statistics, and then to complete her PhD in missing data methods under the supervision of Mike Kenward. Her interest in causal inference started while reading the inverse weighting and double robust estimation literature during her PhD, methods that apply equally to missing data and causal inference problems. Rhian was lucky that a post-doc position in causal inference methods, under the supervision of Simon Cousens and Bianca De Stavola, became available at LSHTM as she completed her PhD, and she has stayed in this area ever since, including after moving to Cardiff University where she collaborates with Daniel Farewell and Simon Schoenbuchner on methods and applications related to causal inference, including most recently their work on Regression by Composition, a new framework for flexibly defining regression models.

title: Vicente Núñez-Antón
text: University of the Basque Country

title: Vicente Núñez-Antón
text:
Short bio
Professor of Statistics at The University of the Basque Country (EHU), Spain, he received an M.S. and a Ph.D. in Statistics from The University of Iowa. He has published on longitudinal data analysis, pseudo-panel data analysis, survival data, nonparametric estimation methods, goodness-of-fit testing, health-related quality-of-life methods, order-restricted testing, spatial statistics, and overdispersion modeling approaches. Jointly with Dale Zimmerman, Philippe Vieu, and Frédéric Ferraty, he has authored books on longitudinal data analysis and nonparametric regression methods.
He has served as Associate Editor for Applied Statistics, Methodology: European Journal of Research Methods for the Behavioral and Social Sciences, and Statistical Modelling. He is currently Coordinating Editor for Statistical Modelling. He was one of the founders and principal investigators of the BIOSTATNET Network in Biostatistics. He was also a founder of the Statistical Modelling Society and served as a member of its Board of Trustees, Executive Committee, and Secretary.
He also served as Chair of the Subcommittee of the American Statistical Association (ASA) Committee on Nominations for International Representative to its Board of Directors. In addition, he has been a member of the International Program Committee (IPC) for the International Biometric Conferences (IBC) in Canada, Ireland, and Japan, and Chair of the IPC for the IBC in Brazil. He served on the International Biometric Society (IBS) Executive Board, the IBS Representative Council, and the IBS Conference Advisory Committee, which he also chaired. More recently, he served as IBS Secretary and Treasurer.
He has given invited talks at universities in Belgium, Germany, France, Italy, the USA, Mexico, Colombia, Chile, Cuba, Ecuador, the Netherlands, and Spain, as well as at various meetings and conferences in the field of Statistics.

title: Ana-Maria Staicu
text: North Carolina State University

title: Ana-Maria Staicu
text:
Short bio
Ana-Maria Staicu is Professor of Statistics at North Carolina State University and an elected Fellow of the American Statistical Association. She serves as Lead Program Director of the NIH/NHLBI Integrated Biostatistical Training for Cardiovascular Disease Research Program, a joint training initiative involving North Carolina State University, Duke Biostatistics & Bioinformatics, and the Duke Clinical Research Institute (DCRI). She is also a member of the Executive Committee and Board of Trustees of the National Institute of Statistical Sciences. Dr. Staicu is a University Faculty Scholar and recipient of the NSF CAREER Award.
Dr. Staicu’s research develops innovative statistical methods for analyzing complex, high-dimensional data arising from modern scientific studies. Her work has advanced the analysis of functional, longitudinal, and spatially indexed data, with applications spanning biomedical imaging, neurological disease, cancer screening, robotic prosthetics, social media analytics, and environmental health. By developing principled tools for modeling, inference, and prediction, she has helped researchers extract meaningful scientific insights from increasingly complex data sources, including medical imaging, electronic health records, genomics, longitudinal cohort studies, and digital platforms. Her contributions have been recognized through numerous awards and leadership roles, and her research continues to shape the development of statistical methodology for emerging data-intensive scientific challenges.
